1: The list of seven nations — Hittites, Girgashites, Amorites, Canaanites, Perizzites, Hivites, Jebusites — is the standard Deuteronomic enumeration of Canaan's pre-Israelite inhabitants. The number seven suggests completeness; these are all the peoples of the promised land. Waḥărēm taḥărîm ʾōtām ("you must completely destroy them") introduces the ḥerem (H2764) principle — consecrated/devoted destruction — which is theologically and historically one of the most demanding elements of Deuteronomy.
2: חֵרֶם תַּחֲרִים (ḥērem taḥărîm) — the infinitive absolute intensifying: "you shall utterly devote to destruction." Ḥērem designates something removed from ordinary use and dedicated to YHWH, whether by destruction or consecration. In warfare, it means the complete removal of the enemy from the land without taking spoil, because the land itself belongs to YHWH and the inhabitants' corruption is total. "You must not make a covenant (lōʾ tikrōt lāhem bĕrît) with them" — covenant-making is the opposite of ḥērem; it would assimilate Israel to the very idolatry it must purge.
3: The intermarriage prohibition is grounded in v4: the danger is not ethnic contamination but theological assimilation. "They will turn your sons away from following me to serve other gods" — the household becomes the entry point for covenant unfaithfulness. The prohibition is directional: the problem is not the foreign person but the foreign worship they would introduce.
4: The causal chain: intermarriage → turning your sons → serving other gods → YHWH's anger → swift destruction. The speed of the sanction (mĕhēr, quickly) reflects Deuteronomy's concern that covenant apostasy does not have a long runway — once the heart has turned, the consequences follow.
5: The commanded destruction of Canaanite religious infrastructure — altars (mizbĕḥōtāyhem), sacred pillars (maṣṣĕbōtām), Asherah poles (ʾăshērāyw), and carved images (pĕsîlêhem) — is a systematic elimination of the material culture of Canaanite religion. These objects represent the rival system that would compete for Israel's covenantal allegiance. The positive command — "burn the carved images with fire" (v25) — makes clear that the destruction is itself a liturgical act of covenantal purification.
6: סְגֻלָּה (sĕgullāh, H5459) — "treasured possession." The root suggests wealth closely guarded; it appears in Mal 3:17 ("they shall be mine, says the LORD of Hosts, my special possession"), Ps 135:4, and 1 Pet 2:9 (where Peter applies it to the NT church: "a people for his own possession," laos eis peripoiēsin). Sĕgullāh is Israel's most intimate covenantal title — not merely God's people but his personally treasured possession, set apart from all peoples of the earth.
7: לֹא מֵרֻבְּכֶם (lōʾ mērubbĕkem) — "not because you were more numerous." The negative election theology here is rare in world religions: Israel is chosen not because of size, strength, or virtue but despite being the least of peoples (haqqāṭōn mikkol hāʿammîm). This undercuts all ethnic pride and establishes election as pure divine initiative.
8: מֵאַהֲבַת יְהוָה אֶתְכֶם (mēʾahăbat Yhwh ʾetkem) — "because the LORD loved you." The reason for election is YHWH's own love (ʾahăbāh, H160) — a circular, irreducible reason: YHWH loves Israel because he loves Israel (cf. Deut 10:15). The second reason — keeping the oath sworn to the fathers — grounds the love in covenant faithfulness across generations. Both reasons are internal to YHWH's character, not external to Israel's merit.
9: הָאֵל הַנֶּאֱמָן שֹׁמֵר הַבְּרִית וְהַחֶסֶד (hāʾēl hanneʾĕmān shōmēr habbĕrît wĕhaḥesed) — "the faithful God who keeps covenant and steadfast love." Three terms define YHWH's covenantal character: neʾĕmān (faithful, trustworthy, H539), bĕrît (covenant, H1285), ḥesed (steadfast love/loyalty, H2617). Together they describe a God whose commitment to the covenant relationship is structurally reliable — not capriciously revoked by Israel's failures but faithfully maintained. "To a thousand generations" (lĕʾelep dôr) is the eschatological horizon of covenant faithfulness.
10: The balancing clause: YHWH "repays those who hate him to their face" (mĕšallēm lĕśōnĕʾāyw ʾel-pānāyw lĕhaʾăbîdô). The divine faithfulness in v9 is matched by a corresponding divine righteousness — YHWH does not ignore those who hate him. The covenant has two faces: blessing for the faithful, sanction for the hostile. "He will not delay" (lōʾ yĕʾaḥēr) — the divine patience has limits with those who actively oppose the covenant.
11: The conclusion of the "faithful God" section (vv7-11): "therefore keep the commandments, statutes, and rules." The indicative of divine election and faithfulness (vv7-10) grounds the imperative of human obedience (v11). The logic is always this order: who YHWH is and what he has done → what Israel is to do. Command follows grace.
12: עֵקֶב (ʿēqeb, H6118) — "because of, as a result of." The term can mean "heel" (the track of the heel, hence the consequence of walking in a certain path). The covenant blessings follow Israel's obedience as the track follows the foot — not because obedience earns blessing but because walking the covenant path leads through covenant blessing. Shāmar (to keep) + ḥāsēd (YHWH's loyal love) — the correspondence of human keeping and divine keeping is the covenant's relational structure.
13: וְאֵהֵבְךָ וּבֵרַכְךָ וְהִרְבֶּךָ (wĕʾēhēbĕkā ûbēraḵĕkā wĕhirbĕkā) — "he will love you, bless you, and multiply you." Three verbs of divine action flowing from covenant faithfulness: love (prior commitment renewed in blessing), bless (material and social flourishing), multiply (generational continuity). The blessings listed — womb, ground, grain, wine, oil, herd, flock — are the fundamental fertility goods of agricultural life.
14: בָּרוּךְ תִּהְיֶה מִכָּל הָעַמִּים (bārûk tihyeh mikkol hāʿammîm) — "blessed more than all peoples." The comparative "more than" (mikkol) situates Israel's covenant blessing in the context of the nations. This is not ethnocentrism but covenant distinctiveness: the people chosen to bear the divine name will be visibly blessed as a sign of YHWH's faithfulness — which is itself the means of the nations eventually coming to know him.
15: מַחֲלֶה מִצְרַיִם (maḥăleh miṣrāyim) — "the diseases of Egypt." The covenant guarantee includes freedom from the diseases that afflicted Egypt — likely including the plagues but also the endemic diseases of the Nile region. YHWH's healing protection is the positive counterpart to the military protection of holy war.
16: וְלֹא תַחוֹס עֵינְךָ עֲלֵיהֶם (wĕlōʾ tāḥôs ʿênĕkā ʿălêhem) — "do not look on them with pity." Ḥûs (H2347) means to have regard for, to spare emotionally. The prohibition against emotional pity toward the nations targeted for ḥērem is not cruelty but the recognition that sentiment-driven compromise would undermine the very purpose of the conquest: not expelling the false worship from the land. "And do not serve their gods — for that would be a snare to you": the warning is ultimately self-protective, not triumphalist.
17: The rhetorical question "these nations are more powerful than I" is addressed before the emotion arises. Deuteronomy consistently anticipates the fear and doubt that will arise when Israel faces the militarily superior nations of Canaan and provides the corrective before the crisis comes.
18: זָכֹר תִּזְכֹּר (zākōr tizzĕkōr) — infinitive absolute + finite verb: "remember carefully/diligently." Memory of what YHWH did to Pharaoh and Egypt is the antidote to fear of the Canaanite nations. The Exodus is not ancient history but the ongoing credential of the God who fights for Israel.
19: הַמַּסֹּת הַגְּדֹלֹת אֲשֶׁר רָאוּ עֵינֶיךָ (hammassōt haggĕdōlōt ʾăsher rāʾû ʿênêkā) — "the great ordeals your eyes saw." The appeal is to eyewitness testimony: the signs, wonders, mighty hand, and outstretched arm are not mythology but narrated history to which the living generation can testify. This empirical grounding of faith is characteristic of Deuteronomy's rhetorical strategy.
20: הַצִּרְעָה (haṣṣirʿāh) — "the hornet." The divine hornet sent among the survivors is a mysterious reference — possibly the Egyptian military campaigns that weakened the Canaanites before Israel's arrival, or a literal insect agent (as in Exod 23:28; Josh 24:12), or a metaphor for divine panic-inducing judgment. Whatever the referent, it represents YHWH's own initiative supplementing Israel's military effort: the conquest is jointly divine-human.
21: אֵל גָּדוֹל וְנוֹרָא (ʾēl gādôl wĕnôrāʾ) — "a great and awesome God." Nôrāʾ (H3372, the Niphal participle of yārēʾ, to fear) means fear-inducing, awesome in the sense of evoking reverent dread. The phrase recurs in Neh 1:5; 9:32 and Dan 9:4 — a liturgical formula for the God who is terrifying in power and faithful in covenant.
22: The "little by little" (mĕʿaṭ mĕʿaṭ) conquest strategy reflects ecological wisdom: complete and immediate depopulation would leave the land to wild animals. The divine timing is not weakness but prudent management of a complex transition. This pragmatic note within the theology of holy war is characteristic of Deuteronomy's realistic ethical vision.
23: מְהוּמָה גְדֹלָה (mĕhûmāh gĕdōlāh) — "great panic/confusion." Mĕhûmāh (H4103) is the divinely-sent panic that immobilizes the enemy, making military defeat possible. The same term appears in the description of divine warfare throughout the OT (1 Sam 14:15; Zech 14:13). It is YHWH's weapon before Israel strikes: the conquest is YHWH's victory first, Israel's second.
24: מָחָה אֶת שְׁמָם (māḥāh ʾet šĕmām) — "erase their name." The erasure of a name from "under heaven" is the reversal of covenant existence: to have a name is to have continuing presence and legacy; to have one's name erased is complete covenant annihilation. This is the ultimate ḥērem sanction.
25: פְּסִילֵי אֱלֹהֵיהֶם תִּשְׂרְפוּן בָּאֵשׁ (pĕsîlê ʾĕlōhêhem tisrĕpûn bāʾēsh) — "burn the carved images of their gods with fire." The fire-destruction of idols is the inverse of YHWH's theophanic fire: the divine fire that spoke from Horeb now destroys the images that would rival YHWH. Tôʿēbāh lYhwh ("a detestable thing to the LORD") — the same term (tôʿēbāh, H8441, abomination) used for Israel's dietary prohibitions and sexual violations: idols belong to the same category of covenant-contaminating objects.
26: חֵרֶם תִּהְיֶה כָּמֹהוּ (ḥērem tihyeh kāmōhû) — "you will be devoted to destruction along with it." The principle of contamination-by-association: bringing a ḥērem-object into the household transfers the ḥērem status to the household. This is not magic but covenantal reality: the object that belongs to YHWH for destruction cannot be assimilated to the covenant community without destroying that community. The story of Achan (Josh 7) is the narrative demonstration of this principle.
